response:
In C# it is possible to measure the time taken for a UDP packet to reach a server  
but it is not straightforward  
UDP is a connectionless protocol  
so there is no built in mechanism to confirm receipt  
to measure one way delay you need to use a timestamp  
you can send a packet with a timestamp  
and then calculate the difference between the sent time and the received time  
this requires the server to respond  
but if the server does not respond then you cannot calculate the delay  
you can use the System.Net.Sockets.UdpClient class  
to send and receive packets  
you can record the time before sending the packet  
and then record the time when you receive the response  
the difference between these two times is the round trip time  
to get one way time you need to divide by two  
this approach requires the server to reply to the packet  
if the server does not reply then this method will not work  
you can also use the ping command in the command prompt  
but it measures round trip time  
not one way time  
in C# you can use the System.Diagnostics.Process class  
to run the ping command and capture the output  
this will give you round trip time  
not one way time  
so to measure one way delay you need to use a timestamp  
and rely on a responding server  
this is not a built in feature of UDP  
but it can be implemented with some coding effort
